content:
A few months ago, I had a conversation with a developer who was convinced that the future belonged entirely to AI models.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Microsoft Copilot on Unsplashread here.
His reasoning seemed solid.
Every week, a new model was breaking benchmarks. Context windows were getting larger. Reasoning capabilities were improving. AI could write code, summarize documents, generate images, and answer questions that would have sounded impossible just a few years ago.
Then he asked me a question:
“If the models are becoming so powerful, why do some AI products feel dramatically better than others when they’re using the same underlying model?”
That’s when the conversation got interesting.
Because after spending the last few years building automation systems, AI workflows, and developer tools, I’ve come to a realization:
The model is often not the most important part of the system.
The real magic usually happens in a layer that most people never see.
And almost nobody talks about it.
The AI Illusion
